The developer focuses on solar and energy storage solutions to support the project's development and financial requirements for this specific energy center initiative.

tech.headline: New cooling panel uses PV and waste cooking oil
tech.summary: Researchers have designed an innovative home cooling panel system. The design incorporates PV power generation, thermoelectric cooling, and latent heat storage using waste cooking oil. Through optimized modeling, results suggest that a single panel can deliver approximately 6–15 kWh/day of cooling, providing a potential new method for residential temperature management using renewable energy.

tech.headline: Chile sets bidding terms for first energy auction including storage
tech.summary: Chile’s CNE has released preliminary terms for the 2026/01 power supply tender. This technology-neutral process favors renewables by excluding high-emission fuels and allowing storage-backed bids. Utilizing 15-year contracts and time-of-day demand blocks, the structure is designed to support photovoltaic and hybrid solar-plus-storage projects, specifically targeting daytime and evening peak coverage requirements.

tech.headline: SNEC 2026 shows solar shifting toward system integration and AI
tech.summary: SNEC 2026 highlighted a significant industry shift from module-centric growth toward system integration. Storage and AI are becoming increasingly central to project design and value creation. At the technology level, back-contact technology gained momentum as a premium platform, while emerging applications and scenario-specific modules signaled a trend toward deeper market segmentation within the solar sector.

tech.headline: Longi develops 27.27% efficient heterojunction back-contact solar cell
tech.summary: Longi and Yangzhou University researchers developed a laser-based manufacturing strategy for HJT back-contact solar cells. This method mitigates shock-wave-induced damage, improving passivation stability and device performance. The approach resulted in a certified 27.27% efficient solar cell, providing an industrially viable pathway toward the fabrication of ultra-high-efficiency photovoltaic technology through improved laser processing techniques.

learn.title: Virtual Power Plants: AI-Driven Grid Management
learn.intro: As we shift to decentralized energy, we need a way to coordinate millions of small power sources. Virtual Power Plants (VPPs) use AI to turn individual home batteries and solar panels into a single, massive power plant. This prevents grid crashes and reduces the need for fossil-fuel peaking plants, making our energy system more resilient and flexible during peak demand.
learn.c1_text: Imagine a conductor leading a massive orchestra where every musician is a home battery. VPPs use AI to synchronize these sources. Instead of one giant coal plant, thousands of 5kW home systems work together to provide megawatts of power exactly when the grid needs it most, balancing supply and demand in real-time.
learn.c2_text: DERs are the building blocks of VPPs, including rooftop solar, EVs, and smart thermostats. By using cloud-based software, these assets can sell excess energy back to the grid. This creates a 'bidirectional' flow, turning passive consumers into 'prosumers' who earn money while stabilizing the local electrical network.
learn.c3_text: India is integrating VPP concepts through smart metering and the Green Energy Corridor. With a massive push toward rooftop solar under the PM-Surya Ghar scheme, VPPs could help India manage the intermittent nature of millions of small installations across residential clusters in cities like Delhi and Bengaluru.
learn.stat1: VPPs can reduce peak demand by 10-20%
learn.stat2: Global DER market growing at ~15% CAGR
learn.stat4: AI can optimize grid efficiency by 5-10%

learn.title: BIPV: Turning Architecture into Power Plants
learn.intro: Why put solar panels on a roof when the entire building can be the panel? Building Integrated Photovoltaics (BIPV) replace traditional materials like glass and cladding with solar-generating materials. This eliminates the need for extra land and transforms skyscrapers into vertical power stations, maximizing energy harvest in dense urban environments where space is a premium.
learn.c1_text: Think of BIPV as 'solar skin.' Instead of bolting panels on top, the windows, facades, and roof tiles themselves generate electricity. For example, semi-transparent solar glass can replace office windows, blocking heat while generating power, effectively turning a 50-story building into a massive vertical solar farm.
learn.c2_text: BIPV uses materials like Thin-Film or Crystalline Silicon integrated into the structure. While slightly less efficient than dedicated panels, they reduce 'soft costs' like installation and land leasing. Modern BIPV can mimic the look of marble or slate, blending high-tech energy generation with architectural beauty.
learn.c3_text: In India's crowded metros like Mumbai, land is too expensive for large solar parks. BIPV offers a solution for commercial hubs. Integrating solar glass into new IT parks and malls aligns with India's Net Zero 2070 goals by reducing the carbon footprint of the construction sector.
learn.stat1: BIPV can reduce building energy bills by 30%
learn.stat3: India target: 500 GW non-fossil capacity by 2030

learn.title: Pumped Hydro: The World's Largest Battery
learn.intro: Solar and wind are intermittent, meaning they don't always produce power when we need it. Pumped Storage Hydropower (PSH) solves this by storing energy as gravity. It is the most mature and largest form of energy storage globally, acting as a giant 'water battery' that ensures the lights stay on even when the wind stops blowing.
learn.c1_text: It works like a giant rechargeable battery using water. When there is excess solar power, water is pumped from a lower reservoir to an upper one (charging). When demand spikes, the water is released downhill through turbines to generate electricity (discharging), converting potential energy back into power.
learn.c2_text: Unlike lithium batteries that degrade after 10 years, PSH plants can last 50-100 years. They provide 'bulk storage,' meaning they can store gigawatt-hours (GWh) of energy. This makes them ideal for balancing seasonal variations in energy production over weeks or months.
learn.c3_text: India is expanding PSH to support its renewable targets. Projects in the Himalayan region and Western Ghats are being explored to balance the grid. PSH is critical for India to manage the variability of its massive 70+ GW solar capacity.
learn.stat1: PSH accounts for >90% of global energy storage

learn.title: Bifacial Solar: Harvesting Light from Both Sides
learn.intro: Standard solar panels only capture light from the top. Bifacial panels change the game by capturing sunlight on both the front and the back. By utilizing reflected light from the ground, these panels significantly increase energy yield per square meter, making solar farms more productive and cost-effective.
learn.c1_text: Imagine a mirror reflecting light onto the back of a panel. This is the Albedo effect. Light bouncing off white sand, snow, or light-colored gravel hits the rear side of the bifacial cell. This 'bonus' energy can increase total power output by 10% to 30% compared to monofacial panels.
learn.c2_text: Bifacial panels use transparent backsheets or dual-glass layers. This allows photons to enter from both sides. They are typically mounted higher off the ground to allow reflected light to reach the back, often using 'trackers' that follow the sun to maximize both direct and reflected exposure.
learn.c3_text: In India's sandy regions like Rajasthan, the high albedo of the desert soil makes bifacial panels highly efficient. Many of India's newest ultra-mega solar parks are adopting bifacial modules to maximize the energy density of the available land.
learn.stat2: LCOE reduced by 5-10% with bifacial tech
learn.stat4: Reflective white gravel increases yield by ~15%

learn.title: Wind Turbine Lifecycle and Blade Recycling
learn.intro: Wind energy is clean, but the turbines themselves have a lifespan of about 20-25 years. The biggest challenge is the blades, made of composite fiberglass that is notoriously hard to recycle. To achieve a truly circular economy, the industry is developing new ways to ensure turbines don't end up in landfills.
learn.c1_text: Turbine blades are like reinforced concrete: strong and durable, but hard to take apart. They are made of epoxy resins and glass fibers. Traditionally, old blades were cut up and buried in landfills, creating a waste problem as the first generation of wind farms reaches retirement.
learn.c2_text: New 'recyclable resins' allow blades to be dissolved at the end of their life, separating the resin from the glass fiber. Other methods include 'co-processing' in cement kilns, where blades provide both energy and raw material for cement, reducing the carbon footprint of construction.
learn.c3_text: India has one of the world's largest wind fleets, primarily in Tamil Nadu and Gujarat. As early installations age, India is exploring 'repowering'—replacing old turbines with larger, more efficient ones while implementing waste management policies to handle the decommissioned composite blades.
learn.stat2: Blade waste could reach 43 million tonnes by 2050
learn.stat4: Recyclable resins can recover 90% of materials

learn.title: Hydrogen Fuel Cells: Zero-Emission Power for Transport
learn.intro: While batteries are great for cars, they are often too heavy for trucks, ships, or planes. Hydrogen fuel cells offer a solution: they generate electricity on the go using hydrogen gas. The only exhaust is pure water, making them a critical tool for decarbonizing heavy-duty transport and long-haul logistics.
learn.c1_text: A fuel cell is like a battery that never runs out as long as you feed it fuel. It combines hydrogen from a tank with oxygen from the air. Through an electrochemical process, they produce electricity, heat, and water. No combustion occurs, so there are no CO2 or NOx emissions.
learn.c2_text: Hydrogen's main advantage is 'energy density.' A hydrogen tank can store more energy per kilogram than a battery, allowing trucks to travel 800+ km and refuel in minutes rather than hours. This makes it ideal for heavy-duty logistics where downtime for charging is too costly.
learn.c3_text: India's National Green Hydrogen Mission aims for 5 MMT of green hydrogen production by 2030. By using solar power to split water (electrolysis), India aims to lead in 'Green Hydrogen,' replacing diesel in heavy trucks and industrial sectors like steel and refineries.
learn.stat1: Refueling time: 3-5 minutes for heavy trucks
learn.stat2: Green H2 target: 5 MMT per annum by 2030
learn.stat4: Zero CO2 emissions at the point of use
